pr.title: Use `objdiff-cli report` to check for regressions
pr.author: ribbanya

summary: Updated the GitHub Actions build workflow so regression checking uses `objdiff-cli report` output instead of comparing aggregate `progress.json` code/data percentages. The new diff-mode path generates actual and expected objdiff reports, computes a changes report, and fails CI when any function regresses from 100% fuzzy match to a lower match percentage.
what_changed: Modified `.github/workflows/build.yml` in the matrix build script case statement.
Kept `link` mode focused on `ninja` followed by `ninja diff`, removing the old progress-file copy from that branch.
Moved the regression comparison logic into `diff` mode.
Generates the actual report with `ninja all_source` followed by `objdiff-cli report generate -o "$actual"`.
Checks out the relevant base or target revision depending on `github.event_name`, then regenerates source and creates the expected report.
Uses `objdiff-cli report changes -o "$changes" "$expected" "$actual"` to compare reports.
Uses `jq` to extract per-function `{name, from, to}` fuzzy match percentages from `.units[].functions[]`.
Fails the workflow if any function had `from == 100` and `to < from`, printing `::error::One or more functions were broken!` and the extracted results.
smart_moves: Changed the regression signal from coarse aggregate code/data progress to per-function fuzzy match regression detection.
Specifically guards functions that were previously at 100% from becoming non-matching, which is more actionable for decompilation regressions than a total percentage decrease.
Stores expected, actual, and changes reports as separate JSON files, making CI failures easier to inspect or reproduce.
Uses `objdiff-cli report changes` rather than hand-comparing old progress JSON, aligning CI with the project’s objdiff-based matching workflow.
Runs `ninja all_source` before each report generation so the report is based on freshly generated source state for both compared revisions.
decomp_lessons: For decomp projects, CI regression checks should prefer symbol/function-level matching data over aggregate progress percentages when the tooling supports it.
A function falling from 100% fuzzy match is a high-value failure condition because it likely indicates a real matching regression even if aggregate project progress barely changes.
Keeping expected and actual report artifacts named consistently under the versioned build root makes local reproduction and CI debugging easier.
Objdiff report JSON can be queried with `jq` to build targeted CI gates without requiring custom scripts.
naming_conventions: Report files are named by role: `expected-report.json`, `actual-report.json`, and `changes-report.json`.
Shell variables mirror those roles: `expected`, `actual`, and `changes`.
The build root is factored into `root='build/${{matrix.version}}'` to avoid repeating the versioned build path.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Uses `objdiff-cli report generate` to produce matching reports for both current and comparison revisions.
Uses `objdiff-cli report changes` to compute deltas between expected and actual reports.
Treats `.from.fuzzy_match_percent == 100` and `.to.fuzzy_match_percent < .from` as the regression condition.
The tactic targets broken previously matching functions rather than generic progress drift.
follow_up_queries: Find later workflow changes that refine the `from == 100 and to < from` regression rule.
Search for uses of `changes-report.json` or uploaded objdiff reports in subsequent CI debugging improvements.
Look for PRs that introduced or updated `objdiff-cli` report schema fields such as `fuzzy_match_percent`.
Check whether aggregate `progress.json` comparisons were fully removed or retained elsewhere in CI.
